The apostle Peter does not command, but encourages. He does not claim power to rule over all pastors and churches. It was a special honor for Peter and a few others to be witnesses of Christ's sufferings; but it is the privilege of all true Christians to share in the glory that will be revealed. These poor, scattered, suffering Christians were God’s flock, redeemed to God by the great Shepherd, living in holy love and fellowship, according to God’s will. They are also dignified with the title of God's heritage or clergy; his special possession, chosen for his own people, to enjoy his special favor, and to do him special service. Christ is the chief Shepherd of the whole flock and heritage of God. And all faithful ministers will receive a crown of unfading glory, far better and more honorable than all the authority, wealth, and pleasure of the world.

Humility preserves peace and order in all Christian churches and societies; pride disrupts them. Where God gives grace to be humble, he will give wisdom, faith, and holiness. To be humble and subject to our reconciled God will bring greater comfort to the soul than the gratification of pride and ambition. But it is to be at the right time; not at your preferred time, but God’s own wisely appointed time. Does he wait, and will you not? What difficulties will not a firm belief in his wisdom, power, and goodness overcome! Then be humble under his hand. Cast “all your cares,” personal cares, family cares, cares for the present, and cares for the future, for yourselves, for others, for the church, on God. These are burdensome, and often very sinful, when they arise from unbelief and distrust, when they torture and distract the mind, unfit us for duties, and hinder our delight in serving God. The remedy is to cast your care upon God and leave every event to his wise and gracious disposal. A firm belief that God’s will and plans are right calms a person's spirit. Truly, the godly often forget this and fret themselves needlessly. Refer everything to God’s disposal. The richest sources of all spiritual comfort and good are entirely his, and the Spirit itself. Then, will he not provide what is fitting for us if we humbly seek him and place the care of providing for us upon his wisdom and love? The whole design of Satan is to devour and destroy souls. He always is devising ways to ensnare people to eternal ruin. Our duty plainly is to be sober; to govern both the outward and the inward person by the rules of temperance. To be vigilant; suspicious of constant danger from this spiritual enemy, watchful and diligent to prevent his designs. Be steadfast in faith. A man cannot fight on a quagmire; there is no standing without firm ground to tread upon; this faith alone provides it. It lifts the soul to the solid foundation of promises and fixes it there. Considering what others suffer is proper to encourage us to bear our share in any affliction; and whatever form Satan attacks us, or by whatever means, we may know that our brothers and sisters experience the same.

In conclusion, the apostle prays to God for them, as the God of all grace. Perfect implies their progress toward perfection. Stablish imports the curing of our natural lightness and inconstancy. Strengthen has respect to the growth of graces, especially where weakest and lowest. Settle signifies to fix upon a sure foundation, and may refer to Him who is the Foundation and Strength of believers. These expressions show that perseverance and progress in grace are first to be sought after by every Christian. The power of these doctrines on the hearts, and the fruits in the lives, showed who are partakers of the grace of God. Cherishing and increasing Christian love, and affection one to another, is no matter of empty compliment, but the mark and badge of Jesus Christ on his followers. Others may have a false peace for a time, and wicked people may wish it for themselves and for one another; but their hope is vain and will fail. All true peace is founded on Christ and flows from him.
